text: A close-up view of the EEA stapler used for anastomosis is displayed, with the handle positioned on the right and the anvil and staple cartridge on the left, visibly separated.

The surgeon places the anvil in the proximal end of the bowel. The green unit, containing staples and a circular cutting knife, is inserted into the rectal stump and advanced to the transection site. The two units are brought together with overlapped bowel, and the stapler is fired to complete the anastomosis.
The EEA stapler places two circular rows of staples and cuts out a circular donut of tissue, creating an open lumen at the anastomosis site.

The surgeon retracts the sigmoid colon upward and out of the pelvis, opening the left lateral attachment first. The left ureter is identified and preserved. The right side is similarly addressed, and the bowel is encircled posteriorly as the dissection continues into the pelvis.
The surgeon uses two applications of a transverse stapler to close and transect the upper rectum, completing the resection of the sigmoid colon.

A small abdominal incision is made in the lower midline, and the sigmoid colon is delivered onto the abdominal wall. The surgeon applies a purse string stapler to the upper sigmoid colon, dividing it.
The surgeon places the anvil of the EEA stapler into the proximal end of the bowel, with the shaft of the stapler brought through the suture line, preparing for anastomosis.
The surgeon removes the resected sigmoid colon specimen and returns the proximal bowel into the peritoneal cavity.

The surgeon identifies the rectal stump by its staple line. The EEA stapler is inserted through the rectum and advanced to the rectal stump, distending the staple line. The stapler unit is opened, allowing the shaft to penetrate through the staple line. The anvil in the proximal bowel is passed over the shaft and snapped into place.

The surgeon closes the EEA stapler unit, approximating the two ends of the bowel. The instrument is fired, placing two circular rows of staples and advancing the circular cutting blade to create an anastomosis.
The surgeon fills the pelvis with saline solution and insufflates the rectum with air to check for leaks, observing the absence of bubbles indicating no leaks.
The surgeon removes the surgical instruments and closes the trocar sites, marking the completion of the sigmoidectomy procedure.
